'OFF CAMPUS' STAR ELLA BRIGHT ADDRESSES AGE CONCERNS OVER INTIMATE SCENES IN PROVOCATIVE SERIES

She explained that while acting is "a joy," the scale of filming the scene would have made it impossible for her to fully inhabit the emotional performance if she had also been worrying about being naked in front of thousands of people.

"I wouldn’t have been able to do the emotional part of the job," she told the outlet. "I’d have been in full-on defensive mode."

Looking back, Headey said she was in a very different position than many younger actresses entering the industry. Her experience on set gave her the confidence to set boundaries she doesn't believe everyone feels comfortable asserting.

GOLDIE HAWN SOUNDS THE ALARM ON CHILDHOOD CELEBRITY CULTURE: 'ALL I CAN SAY IS IT'S A NIGHTMARE'

"By that point, I’d been through the wringer," the actress said. "I could stand up for myself. I’m not saying those actresses couldn’t, but they were much younger and more vulnerable, and had far less experience of being in front of people and the camera and performing. Whereas I was of an age where I’d just tit about and diffuse anything uncomfortable with idiocy."

At the time, Headey told Entertainment Weekly some viewers believed she was "less of an actress" because she didn't perform the nudity herself.

"I've done nudity. I'm not averse to it," she explained in 2016. "But I know I'm a very emotional actor and I get really driven by that. In order to do my job, I allow myself to be really vulnerable. I don't know any other way to do my job. Things really affect me. And the thought of being naked for three days and trying to contain her in the way she would be I think I would feel very angry. I didn't want to be angry. I don't think Cersei would be angry. I did what I thought she would do, emotionally."

Headey praised body double Rebecca Van Cleave for taking on the physically demanding role, acknowledging that appearing nude on camera was far from easy. She also said being a mother and an established public figure made filming the nude portions herself feel like more than she was willing to take on.

"I didn't phone it in," she noted of her performance. "I was actually there for three days with Rebecca."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

For Headey, the controversy also reflected a much bigger issue she believes many actresses faced early in their careers.

"There was this rite of passage all young female actors had to go through, which usually involved snogging and falling in love, and having sex and showing your boobs," she told The Telegraph. "They’d call them the ingénue parts, to make it sound nicer."

The "300" actress admitted she rarely questioned those expectations when she was starting out because she was simply grateful to be working.

"But I just got on with it," she explained. "I didn’t go to drama school, so I would just arrive on a set and be, ‘Oh my God, I’ve got a job.’ And when it came to those moments, I don’t think I even questioned that I should be safe. Instead, I’d go home and cry, or think, ‘Oh, that felt weird and too familiar.’ Now I look back and feel, ‘Hmm, that was rough.’"

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Those experiences ultimately changed the way Headey viewed power dynamics throughout the entertainment industry.

"The weird protection that we offer predatory men in the business because of the disproportionate power they wield, set against the need among vulnerable actresses to work to put food on the table to get the job – it makes me very angry. A job can be completely soured by one person who, for some reason, is allowed to get away with it."

She said the #MeToo movement exposed how widespread those experiences were and believes younger actresses today are far more willing to reject situations that make them uncomfortable.

"The attitude today is, ‘I’m not f---ing doing that,'" she emphasized.

item.title: Sophie Cunningham acted like an adult this weekend while Angel Reese pandered, red-hot Red Sox & Cubs SMACK!
item.description: Angel Reese's "protected species" controversy drew a WNBA suspension while Sophie Cunningham spent her weekend acting like a normal adult person.
item.content:encoded:
'Nightcaps' is a daily column that catches you up on the biggest sports, news and culture stories you may have missed while pretending to do work at your desk. It runs Monday–Friday on OutKick.com.

Whoaaaaaaaaaaa Nellie! What a weekend. What a weird, exhausting, annoying, weekend full of insufferable WNBA antics that make me truly question whether or not we're all just living in a simulation.

Goodness. Thank God SEC Media Days start today, and NFL teams start reporting to camp this week. That means football is close.

ANGEL REESE’S LATEST WNBA CONTROVERSY SHOWS HOW VICTIMHOOD BECAME CENTRAL TO HER BRAND

The World Cup is over, which means we finally get new episodes of Masterchef again. It also means we're one step closer to returning to real football in this country.

More of that, please, and less of Angel Reese. PLEASE.

We'll dive in here in a bit.

Welcome to a Monday Nightcaps — the one where Sophie Cunningham acts like an adult while the rest of the WNBA panders to the #MOB for the umpteenth time. It's so painfully obvious how silly this league is after this weekend, as if it wasn't before.

What else? I've got Ryan Fox winning the British Open in record time, Rory being full of bologna, and my Red Sox refusing to lose a game. Seriously, have y'all seen this run they're on right now? It's insane.

Penultimate Monday of July, folks. Dial it in. Fall is inching closer. Time to start getting those affairs in order for Week 1.

Start working on those summer chores NOW. Get the lawn fixed. Get the garage cleaned out. Check off all the boxes in July, so you're ready to roll in August.

But first, enjoy a Monday 'Cap:

I've covered a lot of silly things out of the WNBA since it became semi-relevant a few years back, but this Angel Reese nonsense I had to write about — twice! — on Saturday is now the leader in the clubhouse.

Imagine being as insane as these people are. You just wake up every day, looking for the next "racist" comment so you can virtue-signal and play the victim. I knew the second I saw the Tempo coach call Angel Reese a "protected species," it was going to blow up.

And then Angel reposted it and dangerously spread a false narrative.

And then the Tempo coach predictably apologized to the MOB, for literally no reason. None at all.

And then, right on cue, the WNBA suspended her for one game. Why? I have no earthly idea. The girl who choked Caitlin Clark got the same suspension a coach did for calling a player a "protected species." Think about that.

They're screaming 'RACISM!' because a coach argued that a player was getting special treatment — which has literally been happening for as long as sports have existed.

Think about how silly these people are, and how silly this league is. It's an unserious league.

Hell, it's not a league. It's a movement. That's what the WNBA is. If we start treating it like a movement, and not a sports league, it all starts to make so much sense.

"Zach, just stop writing about it!"

That's the obvious response, and one I will undoubtedly get later today. I can't do that. If we do that, they win. And they may still win, but at least we'll be able to stand in their way a little longer.

Anyway, I say all that to highlight Sophie Cunningham, who is, easily, the most marketable, most sane person in that insufferable league.

While Angel was spreading dangerous lies on the internet and showing off a level of ignorance that I didn't know existed in a person, Sophie spent her weekend hosting a camp for 300 kids and acting like an adult.

Choose your fighter, America:

If the WNBA wants to market their league and actually grow the game, Sophie Cunningham should be front and center.

That's right. I said it. Sophie Cunningham is the most marketable person in the league right now, and it's not particularly close. She has fun. She doesn't take the game too seriously. She doesn't race-bait or pander or virtue-signal.

She spent her Sunday at a camp playing basketball against 300 kids. They all wanted to do the "point" with her. She wasn't online race-baiting or pandering or acting silly.

What a concept!

She doesn't do the nonsense, mainly because she's a normal person. There's an idea Just be a normal person. It goes a long way!

Let's see if the WNBA takes or leaves that free advice ...

"The WNBA Players Association is currently prohibiting licensees from using the Sophie point, saying that it is "derogatory."

Allllllllllrighty then! Morons.

Good luck to that league. Football season starts in a month.

Thank goodness.

OK, let's move on because we're 700 words in on a Monday and I'm quite sure I've filled my WNBA quota for the day.

Anyone keeping up with what the Boston Red Sox are doing right now in Major League Baseball? Thirteen wins in a row. 18-2 in their last 20. From 6 games out of a wild card spot to 1.5 games up. Only six out in the division.

Incredible:

I buried the Sox earlier this year, rightfully so. The team STUNK. Naturally, Red Sox fan Bill made sure to keep me on my toes over the weekend:

The Tracy Turnaround continues. Who said the talent from Triple A WOOSOX would be called up to turn the RedSox around? Oh yes, me!

The 2nd place YankeesSuck have been swept, now the 1st place Tiny Rays look to be next after losing 4 straight already.

Zach, your analysis has been poor and your commentary on the Sox worse.

Thanks, Bill! To be fair, my analysis was spot-on up until two weeks ago. I did not see an 18-2 run coming, no. Hand up, I'll be the first to admit that.

If Red Sox fan Bill truly saw it coming, I'd like him to email me tonight with the lotto numbers. If it wins, I'll split it 50/50!

OK, rapid-fire time on this Monday. First up? How about the balls on Rory McIlroy calling out Bryson for being "performative" during Friday's second round?

Well ain't that the pot calling the kettle black:

Yeah, come on. What are we doing here? Rory hates Bryson, and that's fine. I like it, actually. Golf needs a good rivalry. It's great for the game.

But don't sit up there and call out Bryson DeChambeau when you bitch and moan as much as anyone on tour. Let's be realistic, Rory. For once.

You know who's NOT performative? British Open champ Ryan Fox, who is, without a doubt, the fastest golfer I've ever seen play:

This Ryan Fox is incredible. Admittedly, I'd barely heard of the guy until this weekend. But watching him over the final few holes yesterday — specifically the last two shots — was just a joy. Guy just got up there and let it rip. No thinking. No lining up. No mulling it over with his caddie. No standing over a 3-foot putt for an hour.

Give me a club and let's get on with it. We could all learn something from that, myself included.

OK, that's it for today. Good start to the week, everyone. We're in the stretch run of July. Let's keep grinding and end this month strong.

And if you're going to a baseball game tonight — especially in Chicago — stand your butt UP during our great national anthem!

Is the Angel Reese story the dumbest thing you've seen today? Email me at Zach.Dean@OutKick.com.

item.title: Cruise ship hit with third stomach illness outbreak in two months, review underway
item.description: Nearly one-third of passengers aboard the National Geographic Sea Bird cruise ship reported vomiting and "abdominal cramps" in the vessel's third outbreak since May.
item.content:encoded:
Health officials have reported a gastrointestinal illness outbreak aboard an Alaska cruise ship, marking the vessel's third stomach bug outbreak in about two months.

The National Geographic Sea Bird, operated by Lindblad Expeditions, was the subject of a July 15 report by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's Vessel Sanitation Program (VSP). 

The CDC said the outbreak aboard the trip was reported July 12 — halfway through its July 10 to July 15 voyage from Sitka to Juneau, Alaska.

STOMACH BUGS SURGING AS DOCTORS SAY CERTAIN TRAVEL ESSENTIALS COULD SAVE YOUR TRIP

The latest incident marks the third gastrointestinal illness outbreak reported aboard the ship since late May, according to the VSP.

In the most recent outbreak, 18 of the ship's 62 passengers — or nearly a third of those aboard — reported being ill.

The predominant symptoms were "vomiting [and] abdominal cramps," the report said. As of Monday, the source of the outbreak is unknown. 

Officials reported that the ship's crew isolated the ill passengers and crew. Officials also increased "cleaning and disinfection procedures according to their outbreak prevention and response plan."

CRUISE TIPPING CHARGES ARE 'OUT OF CONTROL,' SAY PASSENGERS AS COMPANIES TOUT 'EXCEPTIONAL SERVICE'

"VSP is remotely monitoring the situation, including review of the ship's outbreak response and sanitation procedures," the statement added.

The CDC said the cause of the outbreak remains unknown, though norovirus is a common cause of gastrointestinal illness outbreaks on cruise ships.

"Finding the agent that caused an outbreak [meaning the causative agent] can take time," said the VSP. "When an outbreak occurs, people whose symptoms [meet] the case definition are asked to provide stool or vomitus samples."

BELOVED CRUISE SPOT WILL BE OFF LIMITS THROUGH 2027 AS SAFETY CONCERNS LINGER

"In this outbreak, samples are pending confirmatory testing," the report also noted.

Fox News Digital reached out to Lindblad Expeditions and National Geographic Expeditions, which did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Fox News Digital also reached out to the CDC.

Users on an online forum expressed disgust over the latest outbreak.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"They need to use bleach to sanitize every inch of that ship," one commenter said.

"Gross," another person said.

After a previous outbreak on the National Geographic Sea Bird two months ago, a Reddit user wrote, "It happens every year and all the time."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

Another wrote at the time, "My sister and I were on the Sea Bird, on the cruise [that] left Sitka on May 31st. Lindblad told us they were doing extra sanitizing of the ship."

The incident follows a string of cruise-related health incidents that have made headlines in recent months.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

In April, a hantavirus outbreak aboard the MV Hondius drew international attention after it was linked to three passenger deaths.

Also in the spring, more than 100 people fell ill during a norovirus outbreak aboard the Caribbean Princess, according to the CDC.

The National Geographic Sea Bird is a 152-foot expedition vessel carrying up to 62 guests. 

Its shallow draft allows access to narrow inlets and wildlife-rich fjords beyond the reach of larger ships.

item.title: Hailey Ostrom is back defending summer at the lake armed with her golf club and bikini
item.description: Hailey Ostrom crushed golf balls off moonscape in a bikini, delivering a reminder that summer isn't over despite early Halloween decorations.
item.content:encoded:
Believe it or not, the fight against the creep of fall into summer has already begun. The disrespect is at an all-time high. It really hit home for me last July when a Halloween display was out at a grocery store at the beach.

The beach? They're making their money off of summer vacations in July. What are they doing? Our very own Senior Editor of Content Development has already started receiving reports of Halloween decorations in the wild.

My own wife has fallen victim to it. This weekend, while her parents were in town and the temperature was well into the 90s, she came home with a Halloween-themed bag. But all hope is not lost.

POPULAR GOLF INFLUENCER REVEALS WILD STORY OF FAN WHO GOT HER AUTOGRAPH TATTOOED ON HIS ARM

That's where golf influencer Hailey Ostrom comes in. I know she was in Sunday Screencaps taking on Lake Powell, but she stepped up again for summer on Monday, still out at the lake.

Her message over the weekend on Twitter was slightly different from the one she delivered on Instagram. This message was straight to the point, "Just here to remind you it’s summertime."

How do you do that? You do that out on a boat in a bikini enjoying the water. You also do that by dusting off a trusty play from the content playbook. That's what Hailey did on Monday.

JENA SIMS ATTEMPS TO SPLIT THE 'G' BEFORE LEAVING ENGLAND, RAY LEWIS TRAINS IN MMA & MY WAR WITH VERIZON

She pulled out her driver, as she's done before, and she crushed golf balls off the moonscape all in the name of respecting summer.

That is how you fight the creep of fall. Listen, fall is great, mostly because it brings with it football, but let's keep the Halloween decorations in the warehouse until fall arrives.

We do that by not purchasing the early arriving fall items and shaming those around us who do. We also fight the creep by smashing golf balls off of moonscape in a bikini.

Let's take a moment as we wrap this up to recognize and thank Hailey Ostrom for her continued support of and respect for summer. We're only gifted a limited number of them in our lifetimes.

item.title: Inside underground El Paso storm drain system Border Patrol says cartels exploit to smuggle illegal migrants
item.description: Border Patrol says 32 underground entry points from Mexico lead to exits in elementary schools, parks and backyards across El Paso neighborhoods.
item.content:encoded:
Hidden beneath the streets of El Paso lies what U.S. Border Patrol agents describe as "a city underneath our city," a sprawling network of storm drains built for flood control that authorities say Mexican cartels continue exploiting to smuggle illegal migrants from Mexico into the United States.

Fox News was granted rare, exclusive access underground with the El Paso Sector’s elite Confined Space Entry Team (CSET), joining agents as they navigated the dangerous maze of tunnels where Border Patrol says smugglers continue searching for vulnerabilities, despite illegal crossings and apprehensions at historically low levels.

Border Patrol told Fox News it takes smugglers and illegal migrants 45 minutes to an hour to make it through the storm drains, which are hot and tight, with oxygen levels that can change or drop unexpectedly.

The underground system stretches for miles beneath El Paso. According to Border Patrol, there are 32 known entry points from the Rio Grande into the United States and hundreds of exit points scattered throughout neighborhoods, parks and commercial areas across the city.

WATCH: INSIDE LOOK AT THE DANGEROUS CARTEL HUMAN SMUGGLING TUNNELS STILL BEING USED AT BORDER

Jason Hartman, who leads the El Paso Sector’s Confined Space Entry Team, said one of the biggest challenges is where smugglers can emerge once they make it through the underground network.

"There are exit points into schools, into elementary schools, where we sleep, where we play," Hartman told Fox News. "At any point, you can have smugglers or bad guys coming up in your backyard, in your playgrounds, in your kids’ schools."

Just outside one of the storm drain entrances, evidence of previous crossings remained behind. Fox News observed tools, a wallet and even a Mexican driver’s license left near the entrance, items agents say migrants and smugglers often abandon during the journey.

MEXICAN NATIONAL SENTENCED IN BORDER CHILD SMUGGLING CASE INVOLVING THC-LACED CANDY

Border Patrol guards entry points near the Rio Grande, and Hartman says agents rely on technology to detect movement inside the underground network.

"We do have technology that lets us know when they’re in there, where they’re going, when they come out," Hartman said. "Technology grows at a rapid pace."

Still, some manage to slip through. So far this fiscal year, Border Patrol says agents have apprehended 161 illegal migrants that used El Paso’s storm drain system. At the height of the border crisis, Hartman said agents sometimes encountered groups of up to 70 migrants moving through the underground maze. Today, he said, it’s more common to encounter groups of just two or three.

SIX ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS, INCLUDING 14-YEAR-OLD, FOUND DEAD IN SHIPPING CONTAINER ALONG SOUTHERN BORDER

Agents assigned to the specialized team undergo extensive training in a full-scale replica storm drain, learning how to operate in confined spaces while constantly monitoring oxygen levels that can change without warning.

Hartman said the physical conditions alone can be exhausting before agents ever encounter a smuggler.

"For me, it’s the heat and the time and the air," Hartman said.

Border Patrol says because it is more difficult and risky to illegally cross the border, smugglers have dramatically increased what they charge migrants, between $20,000 and $30,000 per person, to guide them through the storm drains.

"Cartels are desperate to try to get people in," said Hamid Nikseresht, a U.S. Border Patrol Special Operations supervisor. "They’re desperate to try to fund the networks globally that they fund because this isn’t just a localized issue. This is a global issue."

item.title: 'Today' suspect caught on camera before hate crime arrest, rap sheet reveals attempted kidnapping conviction
item.description: Andrew Truelove's criminal record spans two decades, including attempted kidnapping and making threats, before his alleged hate crime involving Craig Melvin.
item.content:encoded:
The man accused of a hate crime when he approached "Today" co-host Craig Melvin at NBC's Manhattan studio has a criminal record spanning at least two decades, with charges including attempted kidnapping and trespassing, according to court records.

New York City police arrested Andrew Truelove, 41, last week after NBC says he snuck into a vestibule near Studio 1A in Rockefeller Center and approached Melvin.

A review of footage from the show reveals a man resembling Truelove and wearing similar clothing was pictured in the background as early as 8:03 a.m. the day of the incident. At 8:05 a.m., the man in the background took off a backpack and appeared to place it in or behind a bush outside the building. He could be seen multiple times until 8:34 a.m., when actress Anne Hathaway was on set to discuss her role in the latest adaption of "The Odyssey."

'TODAY' SHOW INTRUDER IDENTIFIED AFTER ALLEGEDLY BREACHING SECURITY NEAR CRAIG MELVIN

According to the NYPD, Truelove was arrested at 8:57 a.m. after he allegedly confronted Melvin with a racial slur in a private area of the building not open to the public.

He was allegedly looking for Al Roker when he found Melvin instead.

In October 2007, Truelove was accused of trying to abduct an 8-year-old from a school in Norfolk, Virginia. He was convicted at trial and sentenced to 10 years in prison, of which six were suspended, followed by 10 years on probation, court records show.

GO HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE FROM THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM

A psychiatrist testified he had symptoms of bipolar disorder and had stopped taking medications after gaining more than 100 pounds prior to the incident, according to The Virginian-Pilot.

Truelove has an extensive rap sheet in the state of Virginia, where court records show he was also accused of trespassing, destruction of property, making bomb threats and multiple probation violations.

In 2023, he served 90 days in jail for the property destruction case. In 2014, he was arrested on charges of stealing signs from memorials dedicated to the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting victims.

PIMA COUNTY SHERIFF ISSUES ALERT FOR KIDNAPPING SUSPECT LESS THAN 10 MILES FROM NANCY GUTHRIE'S HOME

He faces hate crime charges of burglary, a felony, and menacing, a misdemeanor, in connection with the Melvin incident.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

"An individual entered an unauthorized area in a vestibule near Studio 1A," a "Today" spokesperson said in a statement. "The person approached anchor Craig Melvin, who alerted security. The individual was detained and taken into custody by law enforcement without further incident. There was no altercation, and no one was injured. TODAY is cooperating fully with law enforcement as they investigate the matter. NBC and TODAY take the safety and security of our employees, talent, staff and guests extremely seriously. We are reviewing the incident and our security protocols and remain committed to providing a safe and secure environment for everyone who works at and visits our studios."

Court records in New York show man with the same name and age as Truelove has additional pending cases in connection with an alleged assault in the NYPD's 5th Precinct in January, trespassing and reckless endangerment charges on the city's transit system from May — and a 12-count criminal mischief indictment from last year.

He's now being held on $10,000 bail in a New York City jail.

Melvin's colleague, Savannah Guthrie, is the daughter of missing Nancy Guthrie, whose Feb. 1 disappearance remains unsolved. She is believed to have been abducted from her home after Nest video shows a masked intruder on her front steps in the Catalina Foothills of northern Tucson, Arizona.

GOT A TIP?

Anyone with information on that case is asked to call 1-800-FBI or 520-88-CRIME.

With no arrests or publicly identified suspects in the Guthrie case, some web sleuths have pondered a potential connection. However, Truelove has not been linked to the abduction by any law enforcement agency, and he does not match the physical description of a masked man seen on Guthrie's front porch around the time she was taken.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

New York jail records list Truelove as about 6 feet tall and 250 pounds. The masked intruder has been described by the FBI as 5 feet, 9 inches to 5 feet, 10 inches tall with an average build.

The Pima County Sheriff's Department and FBI did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

Truelove has reportedly traveled through the Sonoran Desert region, according to a 2023 profile in the Los Angeles Times that focused on his bout with homelessness on the West Coast after getting off of probation in Virginia.

It was not immediately clear when he arrived in New York City.

Truelove is due back in court Wednesday.
